---
title: "Rendering with code"
format: pdf
---

This a a demonstration of rendering code in a Quarto document in VSCode. The following code block is written in Python, r and bash, and it will be rendered in the document when you run the code block. You can also run the code block interactively, which will allow you to see the output directly in the document


# Install Quatro
Quarto can be installed as an extention in VScode

1. Go to the extensions tab in VScode (the square icon on the left sidebar)
2. Search for "Quarto" in the search bar
3. Click on the "Install" button for the Quarto extension   

![Install Quarto](/project/spott/cshan/fiber-seq/code/test/install_quarto.pdf){width=400}

To render the document, you also need to install Quarto via CLI. You can do this by running the following command in your terminal:

```{bash, echo=FALSE, eval=FALSE}
# create an environment for quarto
## replace /path/to/quarto/environment with your chosen path
conda create --prefix=/path/to/quarto/environment python=3.11
source activate /path/to/quarto/environment

# clone the quarto repository
pip install quarto-cli

# check if the installation was successful
quarto --version

# this should return the current version: 1.9.37

```

To run python in qmd files, it's best to use a virtual environment. You can create one using the following command in your terminal:

```{bash, echo=FALSE, eval=FALSE}
export USE_CONDA_CACHE=0
module load python/miniforge-25.3.0

# replace /path/to/venv with your environment path
## you will only need to create the environment once, but you will need to activate it every time you want to run python code in your qmd file
conda create --prefix=/path/to/venv python=3.11
source activate /path/to/venv

# once you have activated your environment, you can install the necessary packages for Jupyter and IPython kernel using pip:
pip install jupyter ipykernel

# link the kernel to your Jupyter installation
# --name=venv is the name of your environment, and --display-name "Python" is the name that will appear in the Jupyter kernel list
python -m ipykernel install --user --name=venv --display-name "Jupyter""
```



When you run the code, it will start an interactive session with the Jupyter kernel, allowing you to execute the code and see the output directly in the document

# Running python code blocks

1. Once you click on "Run cell", you should see an interactive panel pop up that allows you to select the kernel you want to use for running the code block. This is where you can select the Jupyter kernel that you have set up in your environment


![Select Kernel once you click on "Run cell"](/project/spott/cshan/fiber-seq/code/test/current_kernel.png){width=400}

2. click on the kernel name in the top right corner of the document. For example, the current Jupyter kernel is named "Jupyter-notebook", and you can select from a list of available kernels in the top right corner of the document. Once you select the kernel, you can run the code blocks and see the output directly in the document

![Example Kernel](/project/spott/cshan/fiber-seq/code/test/current_kernel.png){width=400}

 

3. go to python environments, and select the name of the environment you created

![Select Kernel from python environments]("/project/spott/cshan/fiber-seq/code/test/select_kernel.png"){width=400}


```{python, echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

x = np.linspace(0, 10, 100)
y = np.sin(x)
plt.plot(x, y)
plt.title("Sine function")
plt.xlabel("x")
plt.ylabel("sin(x)")
plt.show()

```

# Set up R path

1. To run R code blocks in your qmd file, you need to set up the R path. You can do this by creating a bash script that sets the R path and then running the script before running your R code blocks. The following is an example of how to create a bash script that sets the R path:
```{bash, echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
cat > ~/run_R.sh <<'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
export TMPDIR=/scratch/midway3/cshan/tmp
mkdir -p $TMPDIR

module load R/4.4.1
exec /software/R-4.4.1-el8-x86_64/bin/R "$@"
EOF
```

2. make the script executable by running the following command in your terminal:
```{bash, echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
chmod +x ~/run_R.sh
```

3. Create the json file that will be used to set the R path in your qmd file. The following is an example of how to create the json file:
  - click on the gear icon in the bottom left corner of VScode to open the settings menu
  - click on command platte
  - open <preferences: open remote settings (JSON)>
  - add the following lines to the json file to set the R path:

```{bash, echo=FALSE, eval=FALSE}
{
    "r.rterm.linux": "/home/cshan/run_R.sh",
    "r.rpath.linux": "/software/R-4.4.1-el8-x86_64/bin/R",
}
```


4. Now run the R code block in your qmd file, and you should see the output directly in the document, and the plot will be rendered as well
```{r, echo=TRUE, eval= FALSE}
summary(cars)
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(cars, aes(x = speed, y = dist)) +
  geom_point() +
  ggtitle("Speed vs Stopping Distance") +
  xlab("Speed") +
  ylab("Stopping Distance")  

```


The following is a simple bash code block that prints "Hello, World!" to the console:
```{bash, echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
echo "Hello, World!"

```

# Render document

To render the document, we need to activate the quarto environment previously created

```{bash, echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
module load R/4.4.1
module load openblas

module load python/miniforge-25.3.0
source activate /path/to/quarto/environment

quarto render <your_file_name>.qmd

```
